serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of keema masala is approximately 250 g, served as a main dish alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Keema (Minced Mutton) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports cooked minced mutton (keema) values. Keema masala is a spiced preparation of minced lamb/mutton with onions, tomatoes, and spices. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked minced mutton data with minor upward adjustment to fat and sodium to account for added oil and spice paste typical of restaurant-style keema masala. Energy, protein, and carbohydrate are consistent with ICMR-NIN cooked keema reference. Sodium elevated to reflect restaurant-level seasoning.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for meat-based curry with low-GI spiced sauce
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for keema masala. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous meat-based curry dishes with tomato-onion sauce from the University of Sydney GI Database. Meat dishes with minimal starch and high protein/fat content consistently yield low GI values (GI 30–40 range). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (35 × 4.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2.5 serving factor) / 100 = approximately 4.2 g. GI category is low (≤55) per Veritas threshold rules.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 420 mg per 100 g; restaurant portions may deliver 1000 mg or more sodium per serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit keema masala to small portions and request reduced-salt preparation, as the cumulative sodium from seasoning, spice pastes, and added salt can significantly contribute to daily sodium limits of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated GI 35) and low carbohydrate content (4.8 g per 100 g) with high protein moderating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Keema masala is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions, as its low GI and high protein content support glycaemic control; however, pairing with high-GI bread or rice will raise the overall meal glycaemic load substantially.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content approximately 4.2 g per 100 g from lamb and cooking oil; cholesterol approximately 62 mg per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate keema masala intake due to its saturated fat and cholesterol content from lamb mince; choosing leaner mince, reducing added oil, and limiting portion size to 150 g can help keep saturated fat within recommended daily limits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Keema Masala is a classic North Indian and Pakistani spiced minced meat dish, traditionally prepared with minced lamb (or goat), though chicken and beef variants are also common. The dish is made by sautéing onions, garlic, and ginger in oil until golden, then adding whole and ground spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, and chilli powder, followed by minced meat which is cooked until browned and dry. Chopped tomatoes are added to create a rich, thick masala sauce that coats the meat. The dish is finished with fresh coriander leaves and sometimes a squeeze of lemon. Keema masala has a deeply savoury, spiced flavour with a slightly dry to semi-dry texture, distinguishing it from wetter curry preparations. It is commonly served with roti, naan, paratha, or steamed rice, and is a popular restaurant and home-cooked dish across the Indian subcontinent and in Indian diaspora communities worldwide, including Singapore. Nutritionally, keema masala is protein-dense (approximately 14.5 g protein per 100 g) and moderate in fat (approximately 10.5 g per 100 g), with a low glycaemic index due to minimal starch content. The dish provides meaningful amounts of iron (2.4 mg per 100 g), zinc (3.2 mg per 100 g), and vitamin B12 (1.8 mcg per 100 g) from the lamb. Sodium content is moderate to high in restaurant preparations. Clinically, keema masala is suitable for low-carbohydrate and diabetic-friendly meal plans but should be consumed in moderation by those managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and saturated fat content.
common_user_queries: How many calories in keema masala?
Is keema masala good for diabetics?
Is keema masala high in protein?
Can I eat keema masala on a low-carb diet?
What is the difference between keema and keema masala?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked minced mutton (keema) data, which is the closest approved primary source for this dish. No exact keema masala entry with full nutrient panel exists in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore Foodo at time of processing. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database meat curry category. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for cooked minced mutton. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source. All other -1 values reflect nutrients not reported in the primary adapted source. GI is an analogous estimate (GI 35, low category per ≤55 threshold) based on University of Sydney GI Database meat curry analogues; no direct GI measurement for keema masala was found. Halal status is flagged true as lamb is a halal-permissible meat when slaughtered according to Islamic rites; actual halal certification depends on the specific restaurant. Gluten-free flag is set true as the core recipe contains no gluten-containing ingredients; cross-contamination risk in restaurant settings is not assessed here. The restaurant source (Zaffron Tandoori – Boat Quay, field survey data) is preserved as provenance context only and is not used as a nutritional evidence source.
